pattern: With only three strings, trying every order to merge them collapses the problem to six brute-force merges: merge a pair by overlapping the longest matching suffix/prefix (or dropping one string entirely if it is already contained in the other), then merge the third string in the same way, and keep the shortest, lexicographically-smallest result across all six orders.
hints: With just three strings, it is cheap to simply try every order of combining them -- there are only six permutations.

Merging two strings optimally means checking containment first (drop the smaller one if it is already a substring of the other), and otherwise overlapping the longest suffix of the first that matches a prefix of the second.

For each of the six permutations, merge the first two, then merge that result with the third the same way; among all six final strings, keep the shortest one, breaking ties by choosing the lexicographically smallest.

edge_inputs.description: one string already contains another while the third is disjoint
three minimum-length, mutually disjoint single characters
a chain of suffix-prefix overlaps across all three strings
both other strings are fully contained inside the first
repeated characters make several overlap lengths look plausible
a three-way cyclic overlap where the merge order changes the result

stress.description: random strings over a tiny alphabet to force frequent overlaps and containments
